title: Chasing the Golden Hour (and Finally Catching It)
subtitle: What I learned after a month of waking up before sunrise
excerpt: Golden hour gets talked about like a magic switch. After a month of early mornings I learned it is less about the clock and more about reading the light.
content:
Everyone tells you to shoot during the golden hour — that soft window just after sunrise and before sunset. For my first year I treated it like a rule I was supposed to obey without really understanding it. This spring I finally spent a month getting up before dawn, and the difference in my photos was not subtle.

The biggest shift was realising golden hour is not a fixed 60 minutes. On a clear day the warm light fades fast. On a hazy or lightly overcast morning it can stretch and soften into something that lasts much longer. I stopped watching the clock and started watching the shadows — when they go long and warm, you shoot; when they go short and white, you have a coffee.

A few things that helped me the most:

  • Scout the location the day before so you are not fumbling in the dark.
  • Shoot into the light for rim-lit subjects, with the light for rich colour.
  • Underexpose slightly to protect the highlights in the sky.

The shot that finally clicked

This frame below is the one that made all the early alarms worth it. Backlight, a little fog, and a subject that was happy to stand still for thirty seconds.

If you take one thing from this: the camera matters far less than the hour. Show up when the light is good and the photos will mostly take themselves.
